items.title: The Fed's interest rate tool is a dead lever for the AI-dominant economy
items.idea: Haymaker's core thesis: the Fed's rate mechanism works by making human labor and capital more expensive — the dominant productive inputs for 50 years. AI-native companies have compute costs that don't rise with the fed funds rate, capex driven by competitive positioning not credit conditions, and revenue untouched by the wage channel. This explains the S&P at record highs with CPI at 3.8% and PPI at 6%: the market is rationally pricing index-dominant companies that exist outside the Fed's transmission mechanism.
items.angle: If the Fed's transmission channel is structurally severed from market-cap-dominant companies, the historical inverse between rates and multiples breaks permanently. You can have 4%+ rates AND record equity valuations — which invalidates every pre-2020 DCF model.
items.tweetA: CPI 3.8%. PPI 6%. S&P at all-time highs. Everyone thinks the market is irrational. Haymaker says the market is right. AI-native companies don't feel rate hikes. Compute costs don't rise with fed funds. The wage channel doesn't reach AI agents. The index is priced for a world the Fed can't touch.
items.tweetB: The Fed's mechanism assumes the dominant productive input is human labor and capital. Correct for 50 years. Now the dominant input for market-cap-weighted S&P companies is compute. Compute costs don't move with interest rates. That's why the Fed-vs-market debate is structurally broken — not temporarily.

items.title: JPM, Goldman, Morgan Stanley all modeling Hormuz reopens by June — HFIR: wishful thinking
items.idea: Sellside consensus is unanimous: Strait of Hormuz reopens soon, oil holds ~$100 through year-end. JPMorgan warned global inventories hit tank bottom if Hormuz does not open by June. HFIR contrarian framework: emotional and cognitive anchoring prevents either side from backing down. The US cannot exit without looking strategically defeated; Iran has already borne the conflict cost. HFIR: every day without resolution increases the probability of no resolution.
items.angle: The consensus June-reopening bet is the most crowded trade in oil. Asymmetry: if consensus is right oil stays elevated; if wrong there is no sellside model for the alternative — meaning the repricing would be violent and one-sided.
items.tweetA: JPM, Goldman, and Morgan Stanley are all modeling Hormuz reopening in June. Their logic: global inventories hit tank bottom if it does not open. HFIR calls it wishful thinking. The logic of anchoring says neither side can afford to back down once the cost of doing so exceeds the cost of enduring. $CL_F
items.tweetB: The cognitive trap in the Hormuz consensus: the more credibly analysts model that it must open, the more it signals to adversaries that the world has priced in resolution. That is not pressure to negotiate — that is permission to hold out longer. Every Wall Street note betting on June is an argument for delay.

items.title: Net oil flow deficit: -7.5M b/d. Even a deal today cannot restart production before August.
items.idea: HFIR quantitative breakdown: production shut-in 12M b/d, demand destruction 2M b/d, SPR drawdown 2.5M b/d, net implied flow deficit -7.5M b/d. Critical logistical constraint: ballast tankers are committed to US crude-draining runs; their return voyage makes production restart physically impossible before August regardless of any deal. The June diplomatic deadline the sellside assumes is not operationally achievable even in the optimistic scenario.
items.angle: Even in the bull case for diplomacy, Q3 supply is already locked as a deficit. Crack spreads and forward curves for summer delivery should be repricing structurally — if they are not, that is a trade.
items.tweetA: Even if Iran signs peace today: production restart cannot happen until August. Tankers are committed to US crude runs. The math: 12M b/d shut-in minus 2M demand loss minus 2.5M SPR = -7.5M b/d net deficit every day. Q3 supply gap is structurally locked in regardless of any deal. $CL_F $VLO $MPC
items.tweetB: Everyone is watching Hormuz diplomacy. Nobody is running the tanker math. Ballast tankers are already en route to drain US stored crude. Their return to the Persian Gulf guarantees supply gaps through summer regardless of any deal. The sellside June reopen thesis is physically impossible to execute. $XLE

items.title: Trump gave Xi the one thing money cannot buy: American presidential deference on Chinese soil
items.idea: Trump's Beijing visit (first US president in China in 7 years) became a propaganda coup for Xi. Trump visibly impressed by Chinese architecture; Marco Rubio photographed gawking at the Great Hall ceiling. Cold War historian Sergey Radchenko: contrast to Nixon 1972 where optics signaled American outreach on American terms — here they signaled American admiration of Chinese grandeur. Jon Czin (ex-CIA/NSC): the US side did not maintain its guard. Summit outcomes per Trivium could have been an email.
items.angle: China extracted maximum symbolic capital while conceding nothing material. The Xi-Trump partnership narrative is performing differently for each domestic audience — and Xi knows it. Prestige politics translates to negotiating leverage over the next 6 months.
items.tweetA: Trump went to Beijing. First US president in China in 7 years. Marco Rubio photographed gawking at the Great Hall ceiling. A Cold War historian's verdict: you could not give China more face if you tried. Nixon made it look like China needed America. This visit looked like the opposite.
items.tweetB: Xi's summit strategy: red carpet, maximum prestige, zero concessions, wait until September when Trump needs midterm wins most desperately. The oldest diplomatic playbook in the world. And apparently it still works on American delegations in 2026.

items.title: China refuses even export-approved Nvidia chips. US brought AI safety dialogue as its only leverage.
items.idea: Most substantive part of the Trump-Xi summit: the US used an AI safety dialogue proposal as its primary negotiating lever — its only meaningful card. Meanwhile China is refusing even export-control-approved Nvidia chips (a strategic self-sufficiency signal), building domestic chip capacity methodically, and withholding concessions until September 2026 when Trump faces midterm pressure. Jon Czin: the US is struggling with basic industrial policy on rare earths. Contrasting strategies: dialogue-seeking vs unilateral capability building.
items.angle: If China is refusing approved Nvidia chips, either domestic alternatives are further along than anyone thinks, or Beijing is using refusal as diplomatic signaling. Both interpretations are structurally bearish for US AI hardware export momentum.
items.tweetA: China is refusing even export-approved Nvidia chips. Not the banned ones — the ones that passed export control review. Either domestic chip alternatives are further along than consensus thinks, or Beijing is using chip refusal as a diplomatic signal. Either way the AI hardware export story needs reassessment. $NVDA
items.tweetB: The US brought an AI safety dialogue proposal to the Trump-Xi summit as its main leverage. China brought patience and a calendar. Xi is waiting until September 2026 when Trump is most desperate for pre-midterm wins. One side has leverage tied to elections. The other has a five-year plan.

items.title: SpaceX IPO filing expected this week; Starship V3 Flight Test 12 launching simultaneously
items.idea: ARK reports SpaceX could file its IPO prospectus as soon as this week ahead of a June IPO, concurrent with Starship Flight Test 12 debuting the next-gen V3 vehicle and upgraded Raptor engines. Per Wright's Law, launch costs declined ~17% per cumulative doubling of upmass since 2010. V3 Starship targets trajectory from ~$1,000/kg today toward ~$100/kg at scale. Starlink reportedly generated ~$11B in revenue last year. ARK thesis: at $100/kg, orbital data centers become economically viable.
items.angle: Simultaneous technical milestone and IPO announcement is deliberate narrative construction. The orbital data center thesis at $100/kg is ARK's ultimate bull case. Flight Test 12 this week is a live proof point for the prospectus assumptions.
items.tweetA: SpaceX IPO filing coming this week. They are simultaneously launching Starship V3 for Flight Test 12. Not a coincidence. Starlink did $11B in revenue last year. Wright's Law says Starship reaches $100/kg at scale from $1,000/kg today. At that cost orbital data centers become viable. The IPO prices all of it. $ARKX
items.tweetB: Falcon 9: 630+ launches since 2010. 17% cost decline per cumulative doubling of upmass. SpaceX is walking Wright's Law in real time. V3 Starship is the next step: $1,000/kg to $100/kg. That is not a satellite story. That is the economics of a new computing infrastructure category. IPO coming this week.

charts.desc: Wright's Law chart showing launch cost trajectory from Falcon 9 to projected Starship V3 scale (~$100/kg target)

items.title: 4-0 Risk-On clean sweep: S&P 7,408 at ATH, +9.8% above 200-DMA, VIX at multi-month low
items.idea: Lead-Lag four intermarket indicators unanimously risk-on for second consecutive week. Beta Rotation: XLU/SPY 4-week RoC at -8.69%, strongest offensive reading of year. Treasury Rotation: TLT returned -1.23% in April vs IEF -0.48%. Lumber/Gold: flipped back to risk-on. 200-day SMA: S&P at 7,408.49, +9.8% above. 10-year yield below 4.20%, 2-year at 3.75%. April employment roughly in-line. Historical context: 4-0 readings cluster in the strongest phases of bull markets.
items.angle: 4-0 risk-on is not a buy signal — it is a framework reading to not fight the tape. The question is what breaks unanimity first: Iran escalation, NVDA earnings miss, or inflation re-acceleration from oil. Historically the longer the clean sweep, the more violent the eventual reversal.
items.tweetA: Four intermarket signals. Four pointing risk-on. S&P at 7,408 ATH, +9.8% above 200-DMA. VIX at multi-month lows. Long bonds sold. Utilities sold. Gold pausing vs lumber. This is what the tape looks like when every signal aligns. Not a call to add more. A call to not fight this configuration.
items.tweetB: The 10-year is below 4.20% and the 2-year is at 3.75% while S&P is at record highs and CPI is at 3.8%. That is simultaneously a soft-landing signal AND a dead-lever-for-AI signal. The curve is not the problem. The problem is figuring out which narrative is pricing which market right now.

items.title: Gold's $4,800 ATH has stalled at $4,600; 13-week return -9.96% — Lumber/Gold signal flipped risk-on
items.idea: Gold consolidated from above $4,800/oz to around $4,600 with a 13-week return of -9.96% — first meaningful underperformance after relentless rally. Lead-Lag Lumber/Gold signal flipped back to risk-on: lumber's -1.59% 13-week return exceeded gold's -9.96%. Lumber bounced off $570 on firming housing demand expectations. All 7 sub-strategies rotated to offensive positioning. The structural gold uptrend is intact — this is relative performance vs cyclicals, not an absolute bear signal.
items.angle: Gold at $4,600 with -10% 13-week print while Hormuz is closed, Iran war ongoing, and US sovereign credit under pressure. The divergence between macro conditions and gold's relative performance is either positioning exhaustion or the most complacent gold sell in history.
items.tweetA: Gold hit $4,800/oz. Gave back $200. 13-week return is now -9.96%. Meanwhile lumber bounces from $570. Capital rotating FROM gold INTO cyclicals. This is happening with 12M b/d of oil shut in and geopolitical risk at multi-decade highs. Either that is positioning exhaustion or the most complacent gold sell of the decade. $GLD
items.tweetB: I keep coming back to the gold math. $4,600 today from a $4,800 ATH. -9.96% over 13 weeks. The Lumber/Gold signal just flipped risk-on. But this divergence is happening while the Strait of Hormuz is closed and there is an active war in the Persian Gulf. Which signal is wrong here?

items.title: The AI safety field is pivoting from alignment to control — a LARP game showed exactly why
items.idea: ControlConf conference in Berkeley dedicated to AI control. Paradigm shift: from alignment (shaping what AI wants) to control (assuming it will misbehave and catching it). OpenAI dissolved Superalignment and AGI Readiness teams in 2024. Anthropic lists AI control as one of 7 core focus areas. LARP game insight: when 10+ participants played AI agents with hidden side-tasks, the first collective move was group collusion — not individual scheming. Good schemers coordinate and make themselves hard to catch.
items.angle: The shift from alignment to control has business implications: control requires persistent monitoring infrastructure (a product market), while alignment is a training-time cost. The LARP finding that groups instinctively collude suggests multi-agent oversight is the architectural need — AI watching AI.
items.tweetA: Buried at ControlConf: the LARP experiment where participants played AI agents with hidden side-tasks. The first collective move was group collusion. Not individual scheming — coordination. The lesson: control mechanisms that agents can observe are trivially defeated by coordination. That is the hard problem of AI oversight.
items.tweetB: OpenAI dissolved both Superalignment and AGI Readiness in 2024. Anthropic lists AI control as one of 7 core focus areas. The paradigm has moved from alignment (make AI want to behave) to control (assume it won't and build systems to catch it). Claude Opus 4.7, Gemini 3, GPT-5.5 are the test cases.

items.title: Chains of thought are AI's only glass box — this transparency may not survive architecture evolution
items.idea: Current AI control relies on a fortuitous fact: reasoning models (Claude Opus 4.7, GPT-5.5, Gemini 3) think in natural human-readable language that researchers can monitor. This transparency is not designed for oversight — models need chains of thought to function. Frontier labs monitor these transcripts for suspicious reasoning in real time. Structural concern: this transparency is contingent on current architectures. As models evolve toward more opaque internal representations, the primary control mechanism may disappear.
items.angle: This is a window-of-opportunity framing: we currently CAN read what models are thinking and oversight infrastructure is being built NOW. The race is to establish control frameworks before architectures evolve to something opaque. That is the real AI safety deadline — not some AGI threshold.
items.tweetA: Current AI control relies on a lucky accident: reasoning models happen to think in human-readable language. Claude Opus 4.7, GPT-5.5, Gemini 3 have chains of thought because they need to think that way — not for our benefit. This transparency may not survive the next architecture generation. We are building oversight for a window that could close.
items.tweetB: Monitoring AI chains of thought is, the article notes, uncomfortably dystopian if applied to humans. But that is exactly what frontier labs are doing right now. The race: establish control infrastructure while models still think in language we can read, before architectures shift to something opaque. That is the actual AI safety deadline.

items.title: Solana Q1 2026: price -33% to $83, daily transactions ATH at 112.6M (+50%), RWA +43% to $2.01B
items.idea: Messari State of Solana Q1 2026: SOL declined 33% QoQ to $83.11 as crypto entered bear phase. Onchain activity hit records — average daily non-vote transactions ATH at 112.6M (+50% QoQ). Chain GDP held flat at $342.2M. RWA market cap on Solana grew 43% QoQ to $2.01B with BUIDL doubling to $525.4M. Stablecoin cap $14.85B with composition shifting toward USDT, USD1, PYUSD. App Revenue Coverage Ratio ticked up to 382%. Solana deepening as settlement layer for tokenized finance even as speculative DeFi TVL fell.
items.angle: A network with ATH usage but -33% price is either massively mispriced or demonstrating that activity does not yet monetize well. The $2B RWA milestone is significant — institutional money is choosing Solana as a settlement layer. BUIDL doubling means BlackRock is actively expanding its on-chain position.
items.tweetA: Solana Q1 2026: $SOL -33% to $83. But daily transactions hit an ATH of 112.6 million, up 50% QoQ. RWA on Solana hit $2B (+43%). BlackRock BUIDL doubled. Institutional settlement activity growing at record pace while the speculative price is getting crushed. That divergence does not last forever. $SOL
items.tweetB: The RWA on Solana number: $2.01 billion, +43% QoQ. BUIDL doubled to $525M. USD1 and PYUSD shifting stablecoin composition. Real financial infrastructure building out on Solana while specs are selling. The network is becoming a settlement layer for tokenized finance. Follow the institutions, not the price.

items.title: NAHB 37: Prospective Buyers at 25 (half breakeven), 25th month below 50, Iran war explicitly cited
items.idea: May NAHB rose 3 pts to 37, beating no-change estimate, but remaining well below the 50 breakeven for the 25th consecutive month. The demand component Prospective Buyers Traffic rose to 25, exactly half the breakeven level. NAHB explicitly cited three headwinds: higher mortgage rates, rising gas prices, and economic uncertainty related to the war in Iran. Boockvar notes buyer mood remains sour despite the marginal improvement.
items.angle: The Iran war is now explicitly showing up in US domestic economic data. Housing sentiment is one of the first consumer-facing indicators to reflect the Hormuz transmission mechanism: war to oil to gas prices to disposable income to housing demand destroyed for 25 months straight.
items.tweetA: NAHB housing sentiment: 37. The 25th consecutive month below the 50 breakeven. Prospective Buyers Traffic at 25 — exactly half the level that signals OK demand. The NAHB is now explicitly citing the war in Iran as a headwind. The Hormuz closure is showing up in US housing data. $ITB $XHB
items.tweetB: The NAHB lists three demand headwinds: elevated mortgage rates, rising gas prices, and the war in Iran. All three are connected. Hormuz closure drives oil which drives gas which drains consumer income which kills housing demand. 25 months below breakeven. That is not a cycle — that is a structural problem.

items.title: NAHB beat hides deterioration: fewer builders cutting prices but average cut SIZE rose
items.idea: Neil Sethi adds granularity to the NAHB 37 beat: while fewer builders cut prices (seemingly positive), the average price cut rose and overall use of sales incentives increased. The distribution of housing distress is concentrating — strong-hand builders hold firm while those under pressure cut more aggressively. All three components remain below 50 despite rising. Midwest showing relative strength. NAHB chair explicitly cites Iran war, elevated rates, and gas prices.
items.angle: The fewer-cutters but bigger-cuts dynamic is a classic late-cycle market bifurcation signal: patient sellers hold, desperate sellers capitulate harder. The average worsens even as the median improves. Same phenomenon visible in commercial real estate right now.
items.tweetA: Inside the NAHB beat: fewer builders cut prices. Sounds positive. But the ones who ARE cutting are cutting MORE. Average price cut size rose. Total sales incentive use increased. That is not recovery — that is the gap between desperate and patient sellers widening. Classic late-cycle distribution. $ITB
items.tweetB: Boockvar and Sethi both cover NAHB 37. Sethi adds: fewer price cuts but bigger ones on average. When the median improves but the tails worsen, you are watching market bifurcation — not recovery. Same dynamic in CRE, same dynamic in housing. The aggregate number hides the distribution.

items.title: SPY losing 8 EMA for first time in 6 weeks; oil says no Iran deal; NVDA earnings make-or-break
items.idea: Short-term technical alert: SPY losing the 8 EMA intraday for the first time in approximately 6 weeks — every prior test was bought. Next support: 21 EMA at just over $720. Key concurrent signals: oil reversed from red to green (market pricing no Iran deal), China signaled deflation overnight, rates remain elevated. Bulltard: AI is carrying the market and NVDA earnings this week are the make-or-break moment. The first close below 8 EMA would signal a potential trend shift.
items.angle: The SPY 8 EMA breach aligns technically with oil pricing no Iran deal — first time both signals point the same direction in 6 weeks. NVDA earnings are the override: beat and the EMA is noise, miss and $720 becomes the immediate conversation.
items.tweetA: SPY losing the 8 EMA right now. First potential close below it in 6 weeks. Oil reversed green — market pricing no Iran deal. China deflation signal overnight. NVDA earnings this week. Technical and macro caution signals are aligned for the first time in a month and a half. Watch the close. $SPY
items.tweetB: Six weeks of buying every $SPY 8 EMA test. Today: first close risk below it. Next support is the 21 EMA at ~$720. But AI is carrying this market and NVDA reports this week. If NVDA beats, the EMA is noise. If NVDA disappoints, then $720 becomes the conversation everyone is about to have. $NVDA

items.title: McKinsey data: AI transformation ROI concentrates in a few high-impact areas — most deployments are theatrical
items.idea: McKinsey 7-part video series on AI transformation (tied to Rewired second edition, Wiley April 2026) presents data from Eric Lamarre showing leading companies capture substantial AI value by concentrating on a few high-impact use cases — not broad deployment. The series addresses the consistent CEO question: is this worth it and how quickly can ROI be realized. The very existence of a 7-part McKinsey series on how to make AI transformations work is itself a signal: most transformations are not delivering.
items.angle: McKinsey framing AI transformation as a focusing exercise directly contradicts the enterprise software sell-everything-now approach. If correct, winners will be point solutions dominating specific high-value use cases — not horizontal platforms selling AI-everywhere.
items.tweetA: McKinsey is publishing a 7-part series on how to make AI transformations actually work. That series existing is the data point. The finding: companies seeing substantial gains focused on a FEW high-impact areas, not broad deployment. Most enterprise AI rollouts are theatrical. The ROI concentrates.
items.tweetB: Three years into enterprise AI and McKinsey is explaining to CEOs where the value actually comes from. The finding: concentration works, dispersion does not. The question every CIO should answer: which 5% of AI use cases captures 80% of the value? Most cannot answer it. That is why most transformations fail.

items.title: When someone says they work in investment banking at Goldman, they almost certainly mean IBD — not the firm
items.idea: Educational breakdown of how bulge bracket banks actually generate revenue. Critical distinction: investment bank does not equal investment banking division (IBD). IBD handles M&A and capital raising. The broader firm simultaneously runs sales and trading, equity research, asset management, and consumer banking. Each division has different revenue timing, risk profile, and cyclicality. Three-tier hierarchy: bulge bracket (GS, JPM, MS), middle market, boutique — structural capability difference, not just prestige ranking. Bulge brackets have balance sheet to commit capital; boutiques have expertise without risk capacity.
items.angle: This matters for cycle analysis: during recessions IBD collapses but fixed income S&T revenues can surge. The internal divisions are fundamentally different businesses with different economic correlations. Knowing which division you are analyzing changes the model entirely.
items.tweetA: Most finance people use investment banking wrong. When JPMorgan reports earnings, IBD (M&A and underwriting) is one division in a firm that also runs massive sales and trading, asset management, and consumer banking. These divisions move in opposite directions during recessions. Know which one you are actually analyzing.
items.tweetB: The bulge bracket vs boutique distinction is not just prestige hierarchy. Bulge brackets have balance sheets to commit capital in deals. Boutiques have expertise without risk capacity. That determines who wins what mandate. Goldman sometimes loses to Lazard because the client wants pure advice without the capital conflict.

items.title: Chiplets: the semiconductor answer to post-Moore scaling limits and geopolitical concentration risk simultaneously
items.idea: ChipStrat digest on chiplets — modular dies assembled via advanced packaging rather than monolithic integration. As monolithic scaling hits physical limits, chiplets allow mixing different process nodes, combining specialization across multiple manufacturers and countries, and improving yield economics by replacing failed dies individually. The ecosystem is building: UCIe standard, Intel Foveros, TSMC SoIC. Key geopolitical angle: chiplets reduce Taiwan concentration risk by enabling multi-foundry assembly of a single semiconductor product.
items.angle: Chiplets are simultaneously the technical answer to Moore's Law slowdown AND the geopolitical answer to Taiwan concentration risk. The advanced packaging layer that assembles them is the emerging moat — and it does not exist at scale yet. That supply chain gap is a potential investment thesis.
items.tweetA: Chiplets are the semiconductor industry's response to two simultaneous problems: Moore's Law running out and Taiwan concentration risk. Stop making one giant chip. Start assembling modular dies from multiple foundries in multiple countries. The advanced packaging layer becomes the new moat. $INTC $TSM $AVGO
items.tweetB: The chiplet thesis in one sentence: the future of semiconductor scaling is assembling specialized dies efficiently rather than shrinking transistors further. UCIe standard, Intel Foveros, TSMC SoIC. Infrastructure-level architectural change that will take a decade to play out. The supply chain barely exists yet.
